text: The surgeon identifies a tumor in the transverse colon, preparing to perform a transverse colectomy for its removal.
The surgeon performs a hanging maneuver, suspending the transverse colon to facilitate its removal during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon approaches the pedicle in the medial colic area, revealing the pancreas on both the left and right sides. The lesser sac is entered, and dissection begins from the root of the mesentery to remove the lymphovascular tissue along with the tumor.
Though I am not very fond of using only energy, but we went ahead using only energy and without any suture. So this was basically a suture-less transverse colectomy.
The surgeon has dissected and elevated the lymphovascular pedicle from below and behind the pancreas, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the transverse colectomy.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac and skeletonizes the left-sided colon. Margins are taken on both the left and right sides of the transverse colon. A stapler is used to transect the colon, firing once on each side.

The transverse colectomy is performed, with the surgeon mobilizing the transverse colon further after removing it from the pedicle. The surgical field shows the colon being carefully manipulated to ensure complete mobilization.

The surgeon attempts to anastomose the two ends of the colon using a stapler but finds it challenging, prompting further mobilization. The omentum is removed from the left side, followed by the right side. The hepatic flexure of the colon is dismantled and brought down, while the right colon is mobilized two-thirds from above downwards.
This was essential to get a tension-free colocolic anastomosis.
The surgeon gradually takes down the duodenum and hepatic flexure, revealing the plane above, anterior to the fascia of Gerota. The right colon is visibly mobilized and properly positioned.
The surgeon focuses on the left side, gradually detaching the colon from the omentum.

The surgeon dismantles the splenic flexure, starting from medial to lateral, and mobilizes the proximal one-third of the left colon. This extensive mobilization is necessary due to the complete removal of the transverse colon, requiring both the right and left flexures to be brought down.
The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic side-to-side right-left colocolostomy using a blue cartridge stapler from the patient's left side port. The stapled hole is subsequently closed.

The surgeon places a crotch stitch to relieve tension at the end of the staple line of the colocolic anastomosis. Additional stitches are applied to ensure the staple hole remains unobstructed, allowing it to be aligned with the axis of suturing. An extramucosal continuous suturing technique is performed to secure the anastomosis.
The surgical specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity using a fan retractor, positioned to facilitate extraction.
